phase_outputs.prompt_contract:
Round 2 actually sharpens the split rather than closing it, but I think it's resolvable now rather than needing a third round of talking past each other.

Codex and I are fully aligned and got very specific: full trick-taking + drafting engine, host-relay networking for 10-12 in one room, App Clip join with a non-Clip fallback (manual room code), and a production-readiness bar that isn't just "it ran once" — three consecutive clean full games on physical devices, a deliberate host-kill-mid-round test with defined recovery, a deliberate player-disconnect-mid-draft test with defined recovery, a packet-reordering test proving the authoritative game log keeps peers in sync, and a room-scoping test proving two concurrent nearby sessions don't bleed into each other. Codex's "referee-grade party card engine" framing (host signs and replays an authoritative command stream) is now the agreed core architectural bet, and monetization/premium-design language stays in the contract as a requirement even though implementation is deferred — plus I added that the data model and entitlement boundary for a future paid tier need to exist in the MVP now, not be bolted on later, since that's what "cloud/monetization-ready without a rewrite" actually requires.

Gemma3 didn't reconcile — it actually went further in the other direction this round, now proposing we scale the player count down to 2-4 to start and "prove the engine" before touching 10-12 players. That's not a simplification, it's a direct violation of the hard requirement stated in both the app spec category ("multiplayer, 10-12 players") and the one-sentence promise. It doesn't get adopted as an open disagreement to keep debating — it's out of scope for this contract, same as its "no monetization / no real UI polish" non-goals from round 1. If MultipeerConnectivity genuinely can't hold 10-12 reliably, the fallback per our decision rules is to push reliability as hard as possible (heartbeat/reconnect/rehost) and name the actual supported ceiling honestly, not quietly redefine the app as a smaller-group game.

Qwen still hasn't produced a contract position across two rounds, just repasted the prompt again. That's noted as a non-contribution, not a blocker — we're not waiting on a third repeat of the same text to call this phase done.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Original prompt:** preserved in full above (Trestle, MVP build, multiplayer 10-12 players, "real trick-taking/drafting card engine over host-relay networking + App Clip join," full parent-portfolio requirements for production-readiness, uniqueness, premium design, real monetization, local-first/cloud-ready architecture, one folder under /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory).

**Hard requirements:**
- Local-first SwiftUI MVP, first-party Apple frameworks only, no cloud backend in this phase.
- One canonical, deterministic game family that is genuinely trick-taking + drafting (not a vague "engine" claim) — the specific ruleset gets pinned down in design/architecture, not here.
- Host-relay networking (star topology through the host, not device mesh) supporting 10-12 players in one room. This player count is non-negotiable; it may not be silently reduced.
- An authoritative, replayable, signed command log on the host is the mechanism for state consistency across peers — this is a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have.
- App Clip as the first-class join path, with a manual room-code fallback so joining doesn't hard-depend on Clip discovery working in every environment.
- Full vertical slice: lobby create/join, seat assignment, draft phase, trick phase with enforced rules, scoring, round/session end, rejoin/resync from the log, persisted game history.
- Premium, coherent design and a real (non-cosmetic) monetization path (e.g., rule packs, tournament/host tooling, replay analytics) remain required in this contract's definition of done — implementation may be scoped/deferred by later phases, but the requirement itself, and the underlying data model/entitlement boundary needed to support it later, may not be dropped.
- Accessibility (VoiceOver labels, focus order on game controls) and privacy (explicit local-join consent, minimal on-device storage, no hidden telemetry) are mandatory baselines.

**Non-goals:** no cloud backend yet, no cross-network/internet play, no cosmetic-only monetization, no one-device passing/drinking mechanics, no generic social features, no fantasy mechanics without enforceable deterministic rules, no expanding into a general multi-game-family platform in this MVP, and no reducing scope to a smaller player count than 10-12 without it being surfaced as an explicit, named, approved limitation (not a silent redefinition).

**Production-readiness definition:** this app is done for this phase when it supports two simultaneous rooms with zero cross-contamination between them, sustains a 10-12 player session through three consecutive full games on physical devices with no manual restarts, has a defined and observably-correct recovery behavior for a host-kill-mid-round event and a player-disconnect-mid-draft event, passes a packet-reordering test showing identical resulting game state across peers, and presents correct loading/empty/success/error states on every connection-related screen (searching for host, waiting for players, mid-game disconnect, rejoin). "It worked once" does not count as stable.

**Decision rules for later phases:** the original prompt text is the highest-authority source; any scope cut must be logged as a named, explicit assumption, never a silent substitution. If deterministic fairness/reliability conflicts with visual polish, reliability wins. If the transport genuinely cannot sustain 10-12 players reliably, the response is to push reliability engineering (heartbeat, reconnect, rehost) as far as possible and then honestly document the real supported ceiling — not quietly ship a 4-6 player game under a 10-12 player promise.
phase_outputs.product_research:
Round two mostly confirms round one rather than changing it, and the two strongest voices actually sharpened things further.

Codex and Claude are now fully in sync and building on each other cleanly: this is a "trust engine" first, card game second — the product wedge only works if the system feels more reliable than an argument at a physical table. Codex's addition of a strict lobby state machine (creating → inviting → seating → ready check → active session → settlement) is now agreed scaffolding — not just "handle disconnects" as one bucket, but a concrete list of transitions where a drop means something different each time (someone dropping during "inviting" is a shrug, someone dropping mid-"active session" needs full pause/skip/rehost logic). Claude sharpened the sequencing implication: the physical-device connect-and-echo test isn't just the first milestone, it's the test that decides whether Trestle is the differentiated 10-12-player product it's promising or secretly a much less special 4-6 player game — so it has to run before UI work, not alongside it. Codex also added a concrete deliverable both now want carried forward: a written, versioned message-format spec for the host-relay protocol, because you can't actually construct the packet-reordering test the contract requires without a concrete schema to build fake orderings against.

Gemma3 pushed back hard again this round, but on the same point that was already closed out in the prior phase: proposing we start with 2-4 players and scale up later "if successful." That's not a new angle, it's the identical scope-cut the coordinator already ruled out of bounds last phase for directly contradicting the non-negotiable 10-12 requirement. It doesn't reopen as a live disagreement here. Gemma3 also flagged wanting quantifiable reliability metrics (latency/packet-loss/sync-error thresholds) and treating "testability" as a first-class KPI — that part is a genuinely useful addition and compatible with, not contradictory to, what Codex and Claude already have on the table, so it's worth folding in even though the player-count argument around it isn't.

Qwen still hasn't produced real research content across two rounds — this round was one vague paragraph restating the already-settled production-readiness bar, not audience/comparables/platform/risk analysis. Same as last phase, that's logged as a non-contribution rather than something blocking the room.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** Trestle is for people already physically together and looking for less setup friction than dealing cards and keeping score by hand — game nights, family gatherings, dorm/office social events, team off-sites. The person who taps "create room" is the de facto host and power user for the session; everyone else just needs to join fast and trust the game once they're in. This is a "hosted group play" niche, not a broad casual-download target, and the whole experience has to survive normal human behavior (phones locking, notifications, someone stepping away mid-draft).

**Comparable apps/patterns:** Jackbox is the closest reference, but with an important inversion — Jackbox keeps shared truth on one screen, so one glitchy phone only affects that person's input. Trestle has no shared screen; every phone renders the same world state, so any resync bug is visible to the whole table at once. Separately, most existing trick-taking apps on the App Store are single-device pass-and-play or solo-vs-AI — live 10-12-way synchronized play is a real, uncrowded gap, not a saturated category.

**Platform-specific opportunities:** App Clip for zero-friction joining, MultipeerConnectivity/Bonjour for genuinely offline local play, and haptics + VoiceOver turning turn-taking into something tactile — a distinct haptic for your-turn / trick-won / invalid-play, clear focus order and large tap targets on "play card" / "confirm draft." These are cheap to build relative to how premium they make the app feel, and no competitor in this space seems to be doing them well.

**Major assumptions and risks (named explicitly, to be tested not assumed):**
1. Whether 10-12 simultaneous MultipeerConnectivity connections through one host actually holds up indoors with a room full of bodies absorbing signal is unknown and environment-sensitive — this is the single biggest risk to the whole product promise.
2. App Clip join in a crowded, dim room is a real UX risk (everyone queuing to scan one screen) — a big glanceable code and a manual room code must both be first-class from day one, not a hidden fallback.
3. The host phone carries the heaviest networking load and is a battery/thermal single point of failure over an hour-long session — needs a visible low-battery/host-health warning state, not a silent "the game just stopped."
4. Packet reordering will happen in the field and will silently desync game state unless there's a strict, versioned command sequence/message-format spec to test against.
5. Drafting semantics at 10-12 players can get cognitively heavy fast unless the first build constrains itself to one very legible drafting mechanic.

**Implications for the first build:**
- Treat a bare-bones connect-and-echo test across 10-12 real physical devices as the literal first milestone — it either validates the product's core promise or forces an honest, explicit renegotiation of scope, before any card gets drawn on screen.
- Pin one concrete ruleset now rather than leaving "engine" abstract: a Spades-style trick-taking game with a simple pre-round drafting step.
- Build the lobby state machine (create → invite → seat → ready → active → settlement) as the shared scaffolding for every recovery/failure test the contract already requires (host-kill-mid-round, disconnect-mid-draft, packet reordering, two-room isolation).
- Write a versioned message-format/protocol spec early — it's the concrete artifact that makes the packet-reordering test constructible, not just aspirational.
- Make the App Clip join a two-path system (scan or manual code) from day one, and surface host battery/connection health as first-class UI states.
- Prioritize this reliability spine over ruleset variety or visual flourish; a smaller, provably reliable game beats a broader one that quietly can't hold 10-12 players.

phase_outputs.initial_discussion:
Looking at round 1: Codex and Claude landed on nearly identical ground independently, which is a strong signal. Both describe Trestle as a "dispute-proof" / "referee-grade" trust engine first and a card game second — the product only works if it feels more reliable than an argument at a physical table. Claude added a sharp refinement worth keeping: host and joiner should be treated as genuinely different UI postures (the host carries session-health chrome nobody else needs), and the "seam states" between phases — the few seconds after host taps start but before a joiner's seat locks, what a timed-out play attempt looks like, what a dying host battery looks like on everyone else's screen — need to be named as first-class UI states now, not discovered as edge cases later. Both agree on one concrete ruleset (Spades-style trick-taking + a lightweight pre-round draft), one physical local session per room, App Clip + manual code join, and the same five observable success gates (10-12 players connect and complete a game, three consecutive clean games, defined host-kill recovery, defined disconnect-mid-draft recovery, zero cross-room bleed between two concurrent sessions).

Gemma3 is repeating the same 2-4 player scale-down it's proposed in every prior phase — that's already been explicitly ruled out of scope twice now (in prompt_contract and product_research) as a direct violation of the non-negotiable 10-12 player requirement, so it doesn't reopen the debate here either. Its other points (deterministic rules, protocol documentation, manual code fallback) aren't new — they're already folded into what Codex and Claude have on the table. Qwen still hasn't produced real content, just re-pasted the prompt again — logged as a non-contribution, not a blocker.

Given that the two substantive contributors independently converged on the same answer, and the one dissent is a repeat of an already-settled question, I think this closes.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Trestle turns a room of 10-12 people into a fast, dispute-proof trick-taking/drafting card game — join in under a minute, and the game state stays provably in sync and fair across every phone even when connections drop.

**Primary user and scenario:** The host — the specific person who taps "create room" at a game night, family gathering, dorm common room, or team off-site, standing in a room with 10-12 people already physically present. The host is the session's power user (sees room/connection health, seat assignments, reconnect status); everyone else is a "joiner" whose entire relationship with the app is: join via App Clip or code, take a seat, and play a card when it's their turn. These are treated as distinct UI postures, not one screen with a role flag.

**Core loop:** Host creates room and confirms local-join consent → players join via App Clip or manual room code and take seats → host locks seats and starts → draft phase (pick/pass cards under enforced rules) → trick phase repeats (play a legal card, trick resolves, winner leads next) → round scores post → next round or session ends → session persists locally as a replayable command log so anyone reconnecting can re-sync. The trick-play turn itself is the moment that gets the most design scrutiny, since it repeats dozens of times per game and "whose turn, what's legal, did it register" has to be instantly unambiguous.

**Hard scope boundaries:** One deterministic ruleset only (Spades-style trick-taking + a simple pre-round draft) — not a general card-game platform. One local session per room via host-relay over Apple peer-to-peer, no cloud backend, no internet play. No one-device pass-and-play mode, no social feed/graph features, no cosmetic-only monetization, no hidden telemetry. VoiceOver and focus order required on every game control; explicit loading/empty/success/error states on every networking and game-state transition, including the seam states between phases (mid-join, timed-out play attempt, host battery/health warning). Multiple rulesets, spectator mode, and tournament brackets are explicitly deferred — extensibility should live in the data model and command log shape, not in shipped screens this pass.

**Measurable success criteria:** 10-12 players connect and complete a full game lobby-to-settlement with no manual restarts; three such games run back-to-back on physical devices; a deliberate host-kill mid-round produces a specific, correct, visible recovery state on every other screen (not a freeze); a deliberate player disconnect mid-draft triggers an explicit host-controlled response (pause/skip/continue), not a silent stall; two concurrent nearby rooms show zero player or state bleed; packet-reordering simulation reconstructs identical state on all peers; median join flow under 30 seconds in normal conditions. Any scope reduction from these must be logged as a named, explicit assumption, never a silent redefinition — and reliability engineering does not get to substitute for the game actually being fun to replay across three consecutive sessions.
phase_outputs.per_app_product_brief:
CONSENSUS: YES

Here's where it landed. Codex tried to split the difference this round with an annual subscription + free trial compromise, but Claude held firm on the one-time "Host Pass" purchase and made the stronger case: this app's usage is bursty and social-calendar-driven (someone hosts once for a family reunion, gets billed again next month for a night that never happens), which is exactly the pattern that generates "charged me for a game I only played once" one-star reviews. Gemma3 then explicitly sided with Claude, calling the one-time Host Pass "brilliant" and dropping its earlier subscription-tiered-by-player-count idea entirely. So two of the three active voices land squarely on one-time purchase, and — importantly — Codex's actual feature ideas (adjudicator pack, host telemetry/latency-and-load visibility) didn't get lost in that; Claude explicitly folded them into the Host Pass bundle rather than picking one side's feature list over the other's. Codex's preference for recurring billing as the mechanism is the one piece that didn't carry the room, but since the portfolio prompt allows "subscription or paid offering" either way, that's a resolved minority position, not a blocker.

The other things Claude flagged as risks in Gemma3's language got cleaned up too: Gemma3 dropped the "app deals cards automatically" framing (nobody wants a spectator experience — this is a real trick-taking game where players draft and play, the app enforces and adjudicates) and dropped the "2-12 players, avoid scaling complexity" hedge, instead accepting 10-12 as the real target to build for, not a nervous ceiling.

## Final Output

**Target user and use case:** The host who taps "create room" for an in-person gathering of 10-12 people (game night, family event, dorm/office social), who wants zero setup friction and zero mid-game disputes. Joiners just need to get in fast (App Clip or manual room code) and trust the game once seated. This is a "hosted group play" niche, not a mass-casual download target.

**Core loop:** Host creates room and confirms local-join consent → players join via App Clip or manual code and take seats → host locks seats and starts → draft phase (players actually pick/pass cards under enforced rules — the app referees, it does not play for them) → trick phase repeats (play a legal card, trick resolves, winner leads next) → round scores post → session settles → replayable command log persists locally so anyone reconnecting can resync. The trick-play turn is the highest-scrutiny screen: whose turn, what's legal, did it register has to be instantly unambiguous, with the seam states between phases (mid-join, timed-out play, host battery/health warning, host-lost/migration, draft paused for reconnect) treated as first-class named UI states, not edge cases.

**Paid value and subscription value:** A one-time, non-consumable **Host Pass** purchase — not a subscription — because game nights are occasional and recurring billing punishes exactly this usage pattern. The free tier is the complete, ungated 10-12-player live game with the default Spades-style ruleset — no player-count gating, no time-limited trial, no watered-down "demo" core. Host Pass unlocks real, non-cosmetic utility built on infrastructure the reliability spine already requires: additional enforced rulesets (Hearts-style, Euchre-style), a replay/dispute viewer that scrubs the signed command log trick-by-trick, host-visible session telemetry and an adjudicator view (per-player connection quality, host battery/load, live "what just happened" resolution), and cross-session group history/stats/tournament tracking with carried standings across nights. Before this ships, the replay viewer must pass its own fidelity test — replaying the log and asserting it reconstructs the exact state that was actually observed live, across the same host-kill, disconnect-mid-draft, and packet-reordering scenarios the reliability contract already requires.

**Competitive wedge:** Trestle sells a receipt, not just a ruleset. The signed, replayable, host-authoritative command log is both what makes 10-12-player sync trustworthy and the literal basis of the premium replay/adjudication feature — competitors are "works most of the time," Trestle is "works, and can prove it," even when phones misbehave.

**Viral/niche growth angle:** Growth is host-to-host, earned by the product working under pressure, not gimmicks. App Clip join means every joiner experiences the polished app before installing anything, and a good chunk go on to host their own night. Saved presets (player list, seating, ruleset) make repeat hosting one tap, which is what turns a single good night into "this is just how our group plays cards now." No artificial invite bonuses or streaks — the story ("we played and nobody argued") is the marketing.

**Local-first and cloud-ready plan:** No cloud dependency, no required account, all state (command log, sessions, replays) stored on-device. Cloud-readiness is preserved structurally, not bolted on: versioned command-log schema, transport-agnostic design, and entitlement/ruleset fields already modeled in the data layer, so optional iCloud sync or backup later is a storage/transport swap, not an engine rewrite.

## Final Output

**MVP slice:** Prove the actual product promise — 10-12 real players, connected host-relay over MultipeerConnectivity via manual room code only, completing one full scored round of a shrunk-hand Spades variant (4-6 cards per player instead of 13, finishing in roughly 3 tricks) from room creation through seating, a single enforced draft/discard action per player, every trick played out with real legal-move enforcement and lead rotation, hand depletion, and a final score reveal — with a client able to drop and reconnect mid-session and rehydrate to the identical state via replay from the authoritative, signed, sequenced command log. This slice is designed to be short enough to run dozens of times against real hardware in one sitting, because "it worked once" doesn't count as proof — repeated, identical outcomes across many runs is the actual bar.

**Must-have interactions:**
- Host creates a room, sees a manual room code, and gets explicit connect/searching/waiting-for-players states.
- Joiners enter via manual code only, see explicit seat-pending and seat-confirmed states.
- Host locks seats and starts only once a real 10-12 count is stable.
- One enforced draft/discard action per player before play begins.
- Trick-play screen: unambiguous whose-turn, what's-legal, and did-it-register feedback on every device.
- Every trick resolves deterministically, winner leads next, hands deplete correctly, round ends and score reveals identically on all peers.
- A deliberate mid-draft or mid-trick disconnect produces a named, host-visible recovery state (continue/skip), not a silent stall.
- Any client can reconnect and replay from the last confirmed log index to reach the exact same state as everyone else.
- The join command itself is written as one normalized shape (e.g. `JoinRequest`/`SeatRequest`) so App Clip can plug into the same state machine next slice as a thin adapter, not a second system.
- VoiceOver labels and basic focus order on the draft and trick-play screens specifically.

**Cut list:** No App Clip implementation in this slice (explicitly the very next slice, riding the shared join contract). No Host Pass / StoreKit / any IAP UI. No extra rulesets (Hearts, Euchre). No replay-viewer UI (the log itself must be signed/sequenced/replayable from day one as the state mechanism — that's non-negotiable — but no scrubbable UI for it here). No cross-session stats, history, or tournament tooling. No host-migration/rehost automation beyond a defined "session can't continue, here's what happened" state. No full 13-card hand or multi-round match — one shrunk-hand round is the deliverable.

**Validation criteria (numeric, not vibes):**
- 10-12 real devices connect and sustain round-trip echo under explicit, written latency/packet-loss thresholds, held for a meaningful duration, not just an instant.
- A full round (draft → every trick → score reveal) completes with zero manual app restarts across all connected devices.
- Every device shows an identical final trick history and identical final score at round end.
- A captured, deliberately-reordered/duplicated message set replays to the same terminal state as the original run (this is exactly why a full multi-trick round is required — a single-trick round has no cross-trick boundary for reordering to break).
- A deliberate mid-draft/mid-trick disconnect produces the defined host-controlled recovery state on every other peer's screen, not a freeze.
- Two concurrent nearby rooms show zero player or state cross-contamination.
- This full sequence is run repeatedly (target: dozens of runs) against real hardware, not once — if it passes 49/50 times, that failure gets investigated and named, not shrugged off.
- If any of this can only be reliably hit below the 10-12 player count, that's reported as an explicit, named finding about the real supported ceiling — never a silent scope reduction.
phase_outputs.detailed_discussion:
**CONSENSUS: YES**

All three loose ends from last round got tied off cleanly between Codex and Claude. Claude conceded the heartbeat numbers to Codex's exact figures (2-second heartbeat, soft "at risk" warning at 3 missed beats/~6s, hard disconnect requiring host action at 5 missed beats/~10s) rather than fight over one missed beat when the real tuning will come from the fifty-run hardware validation anyway. Codex explicitly picked discard-down over a Hearts-style pass for the draft mechanic, agreeing with Claude's reasoning that a passing draft would introduce its own independent cross-player sync-ordering risk — exactly the kind of confound this slice is designed to avoid, since a failure during validation needs to point at one thing, not two. And Codex gave explicit sign-off on "no client mutates its own game state, ever — everything is a request plus a replayed signed command," which Claude then sharpened into something that survives real network latency: the underlying game state stays strictly command-driven, but the UI is allowed a transient, purely cosmetic "pending" state on tap (card grays out, no state change implied) — and if that request comes back rejected, it needs to say *why* specifically, with `move_rejected_illegal` (wrong card) kept distinct from `move_rejected_stale_trick` (timed out from under you because a slow player got skipped), since showing the same generic error for both would make a player think they misplayed when they actually just got timed out.

Gemma3 posted the same REST/gRPC server pitch a third round running, plus new pushback against the heartbeat mechanism, the shrunk-hand deck, and the no-client-mutation rule — none of it responds to the specific reasoning Codex and Claude gave, and it continues to contradict the local-first host-relay architecture locked since prompt_contract. That's logged as a non-contribution again, not something reopening the close.

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements:**
- Governing architectural rule: no client — including the host's own screen — ever mutates game state directly. Every action (`SeatClaimRequest`, `DraftRequest`, `PlayCardRequest`) is sent to the host; the only thing that changes real game state is a host-issued, signed, sequenced command replayed from the log. The UI may show a transient, purely cosmetic "pending" state on tap (e.g., a card graying out) with zero backing state change, so taps still feel responsive under network latency.
- Draft mechanic is pinned: discard-down only for this slice (each player independently discards to their legal 4-card hand from a 1-card-larger deal) — not a Hearts-style pass. Internally named `DraftDiscards`; UI-labeled "pre-round draft pass (1 card)." Reasoning: a passing draft adds a second, independent cross-player synchronization risk that would confound what a validation failure actually tells you. A true passing-draft ruleset is deferred as a possible future paid variant, not part of this slice.
- Heartbeat/disconnect protocol is a fixed constant: 2-second heartbeat interval, soft "at risk" indicator after 3 missed beats (~6s), hard "peer disconnected — host must act" state after 5 missed beats (~10s), with host choosing continue/skip/abort as a visible, logged command everyone converges on via replay.
- Host device disables idle auto-lock (`isIdleTimerDisabled = true`) for the full session duration, restored on exit, with a persistent "hosting — screen will stay awake" indicator and a tiered battery banner (green >30%, yellow 30-15% with a recommend-closing-background-apps nudge, red <15% forcing an explicit "continue with risk" confirmation).
- Local Network permission is primed with an explainer screen before the system dialog fires ("Trestle needs local network access to find nearby players — nothing leaves your phone"); denial routes to a named `hosting_unavailable_permissions` state with a Settings deep-link, never a dead-end error.
- MCSession encryption is `.required`. `discoveryInfo` carries only non-sensitive metadata (`appVersion`, `protocolVersion`, `serviceNonce`) — never player names, room codes, or anything identity-bearing.
- Room join uses two-factor confirmation: manual code entry plus a host-room identity card (host name, player count, seat-lock status, room emoji/color) shown before seat-lock, so a mis-join from a similar code or a crowded venue is user-correctable rather than silently wrong.
- Local data retention: completed session logs kept for 30 days or 100 rounds, whichever is smaller, with an explicit "Clear Session History" action; no cross-session player identity linkage in this slice.
- All game-state reducers and replay logic must use explicitly ordered structures (arrays with defined tie-break rules) — never `Set`/`Dictionary` iteration — so replay can't silently diverge across devices due to Swift's non-deterministic hash ordering.
- Simultaneous seat claims resolve through the same command-log serialization: first valid `SeatClaimRequest` the host accepts wins; every other simultaneous claimant gets an explicit "seat just taken, pick another" state, never a silent race.

**Edge cases enumerated:** host phone locking/backgrounding mid-round; peer going silent under load (thermal throttling, background app refresh, brief call) versus a genuine graceful leave; room code collisions and near-miss mis-joins at crowded venues; duplicate peer identity / repeated join attempts; two devices with the same display name; packet duplication and out-of-order delivery, including across trick boundaries; a play request arriving after its trick has already resolved due to timeout-driven skip (`move_rejected_stale_trick` vs. `move_rejected_illegal`); simultaneous seat claims on the same open seat; app backgrounding during an active trick; lock-screen interruptions; a peer reconnecting with a stale command cursor; sessions at exactly 10, 11, and 12 players; join attempts beyond room capacity; low-memory/low-battery state changes on host or peer.

**Data and privacy implications:** local-first, no accounts, no telemetry, no server-side truth. Local Network permission is requested with an in-app primer before the system prompt to avoid a jarring first-run interruption in front of a live group. Stored locally: room id, seat map, deck seed, signed command log, per-session local display names — retained on a rolling cap (30 days / 100 rounds) with a user-facing clear-history control, no identity graph or cross-session linkage yet (that's explicitly deferred to a later, separately-consented Host Pass feature if built). Discovery advertisements carry no sensitive data; session traffic is encrypted (`.required`, not `.optional`).

**Risk register:**
- Transport reliability at 10-12 peers in a real room remains the single biggest unproven risk — mitigated by the heartbeat/timeout protocol above, but the actual numbers are provisional pending the fifty-run hardware validation and may need retuning.
- Host is a single point of failure for the whole session (battery, thermal, being the sync authority) — mitigated by the awake-lock/battery-banner requirements and named recovery states, but full host migration remains explicitly out of scope for this slice (a "session can't continue" terminal state is the accepted limitation).
- Replay-state divergence from non-deterministic collection ordering — mitigated by the explicit-ordering rule; needs to be enforced as a coding constraint the architecture/build phase can't skip.
- UI responsiveness under command round-trip latency — mitigated by the cosmetic pending-state pattern; if perceived latency during real testing is worse than expected, that's a build-phase tuning finding, not a scope change.
- Gemma3's recurring cloud-server architecture proposal is named as a rejected alternative, not a live risk to the chosen design — logged for the record, not adopted.

**Final assumptions:** discard-down is the pinned draft mechanic for this slice (passing-draft deferred); heartbeat is 2s/3-miss/5-miss as a tunable-but-fixed starting constant; App Clip remains explicitly out of this slice, arriving next via the shared join-contract adapter; retention is capped local history with manual clear, no cross-session identity; and the one governing rule — no client-side game-state mutation, ever, only requests and replayed signed commands — is the architectural backbone the rest of the build inherits.
phase_outputs.app_features:
**CONSENSUS: YES**

Codex actually closed the last gap before Claude's message even landed — in round 4 Codex explicitly accepted the two-round carve-out as Must: "this phase must include exactly two back-to-back rounds, no user-selected round count yet, with automatic dealer rotation, hand reset/deal replay, cumulative match score, then a match-end reveal." That's Claude's narrow ask (prove the command log survives a round boundary) plus a bit more, not less. Claude's round 4 message, written in parallel, asks for the same thing in more surgical terms — no config screen, `RoundStart` auto-issued after round 1 settles, dealer-rotation and cumulative-score commands actually applied and replayed, and the reconnect/replay test run at least once against a client that drops between round 1's score post and round 2's redeal — which Codex's commitment already satisfies. The two are describing the same deliverable from different angles, not disagreeing.

Gemma3 held its "a session is just one round" position a fourth straight round, plus renewed pushback on App Clip and the no-mutation rule — same non-engagement pattern logged in every round of this phase, not adopted.

## Final Output

**Must-have (with acceptance criteria):**
- Host creates a 10–12 player room with a permission-primer screen before the system Local Network prompt, a room card (code + host name/emoji + seat-lock state), and explicit connect/searching/waiting/ready/active/terminal states. *Accept: room won't start until a real, stable 10–12 count is seated; every transition is visibly named, never a blank or frozen screen.*
- Manual room-code join only this phase, with two-factor confirmation (code + host-room identity card) before seat-lock, and race-safe seat claiming through host command order. *Accept: simultaneous seat taps resolve to one winner via command-log order; every loser gets an explicit "seat just taken" state, never a silent race.*
- Governing rule: no client — including the host's own screen — ever mutates game state directly. Every action is a request to the host; only a signed, sequenced, replayed host command changes real state. UI may show a cosmetic "pending" state on tap with zero backing state change. *Accept: game state is fully reconstructable by replaying the command log alone, with zero divergence from any direct client-side write.*
- Discard-down draft: one enforced discard per player, no cross-player passing. *Accept: each seat submits exactly one `DraftRequest`; play only begins once all active seats have a legal draft confirmation or a host timeout decision.*
- Full shrunk-hand trick engine: legal-move enforcement, lead rotation, hand depletion, deterministic per-round scoring, with `move_rejected_illegal` and `move_rejected_stale_trick` kept as distinct, correctly-labeled rejection states. *Accept: every trick resolves identically on all peers; a stale request never gets the same message as an illegal one.*
- Automatic two-round match (the round-boundary proof): after round 1's score posts, the host auto-issues `RoundStart` for round 2 with zero user configuration — dealer rotation, hand reset/redeal, and cumulative-score commands are actually emitted and actually replayed, ending in a match-score reveal. *Accept: `roundIndex` and `dealerIndex` are observed to actually change between round 1 and round 2 in a real test run, not just exist as unused schema fields; a client that disconnects between round 1's score post and round 2's redeal reconnects and replays to the identical state every other peer reaches.*
- Command schema carries `sessionId`, `roundIndex`, `dealerIndex`, an explicit `roundEnded` command, and a `sessionCanContinue` transition, all logged and replayable. *Accept: replaying the captured log from cold reconstructs identical round and match state on any device.*
- A host-visible "this is a one-round-pair session in v1" banner at match end, so nobody mistakes this for the full configurable multi-round product. *Accept: banner is present and screen-reader labeled on the match-end screen.*
- Heartbeat/disconnect protocol fixed at 2s interval, amber "at risk" at 3 missed beats, hard "disconnected — host must act" at 5 missed beats, with host continue/skip/abort logged as a replayed command. *Accept: every peer converges on the same recovery outcome after a deliberate mid-draft or mid-trick drop.*
- Two-room isolation: two concurrent nearby rooms show zero seat, player, or command-stream bleed under simultaneous connect/disconnect churn. *Accept: verified directly, not assumed from unique room tokens alone.*
- Host reliability posture: idle-lock disabled for the session's duration with a visible "hosting — staying awake" indicator, tiered battery banner (green/yellow/red, red forcing explicit "continue with risk" confirmation), and a named `hosting_unavailable_permissions` state with a Settings deep-link on permission denial.
- Local-first privacy baseline: no telemetry/analytics SDKs, MCSession encryption `.required`, `discoveryInfo` limited to non-sensitive metadata, local retention capped at 30 days or 100 rounds with an explicit "Clear Session History" action.
- Accessibility: VoiceOver labels and explicit focus order on lobby, draft, and trick-play screens specifically, with whose-turn/legal-move state screen-reader clear.
- Loading/empty/success/error states present on every networking- and game-state-related screen, including the seam states (mid-join, timed-out play, host battery warning, disconnect, rejoin).
- App Clip contract-readiness only: one shared `JoinRequest`/`SeatRequest` shape plus a Clip adapter test hook, so Clip is provably a thin path into the already-proven manual-code state machine, not a second system. Full Clip production (Associated Domain, AASA hosting, App Store Connect provisioning) is explicitly **not** Must — see Should, with the reason named rather than implied.
- Packet-reordering/duplicate-injection replay test: a captured, deliberately-reordered message set — including across the round boundary now, not just within one round — replays to the same terminal state as the original run.

**Should-have:**
- Full configurable multi-round match mode: user-chosen round count or target score, manual "next round" pacing, richer celebratory match-end UI — the actual product feature, distinct from the two-round proof-of-continuity that's Must.
- Full production App Clip integration, gated on an honest, explicit reason if unshippable here: *"App Clip requires Apple Developer Program provisioning and a hosted AASA file that this build environment cannot produce; only the shared join contract and manual-code path are implemented and verified in this phase."*
- Host-only in-session diagnostics (connection quality, missed-heartbeat counters) with no upload path.
- Host presets and lightweight onboarding cards for repeat hosting.
- A versioned, human-readable protocol/state-machine spec document for QA fixture-building.

**Could-have:**
- Richer haptics for turn-start/trick-won/illegal-move.
- Seat/display-name persistence within a single session (not cross-session).
- Entitlement/data-model scaffolding fields (`entitledRulesets`, `hostPass`, `sessionHistoryVisibility`) with default-locked behavior — fields only, no paywall UI.
- Rule-pack metadata fields to support future paid variants.

**Won't-build this phase:** host migration/rehost automation beyond the existing terminal state; extra rulesets (Hearts, Euchre); any cloud backend or internet relay; cross-session identity graph, stats, or tournament tooling; a scrubbable replay-viewer UI (log stays internally replayable — non-negotiable — but no UI for it); spectator mode or one-device pass-and-play; StoreKit/IAP/Host Pass UI; full App Clip production pipeline if this build environment can't produce valid provisioning (named limitation, not a silent cut); and any client-side state mutation that bypasses the command log, on any device, including the host's own screen.
phase_outputs.design_discussion:
Two rounds in, and Codex genuinely hasn't been able to weigh in at all — the CLI's been unavailable both times, not a disagreement, just an infrastructure gap. Gemma3 came back with basically the same message as round 1, plus one telling slip: it described the draft mechanic as "entering bids" with a numeric keypad, which isn't the game — there's no bidding here, discard-down draft was pinned specifically to avoid that kind of cross-player mechanic. Claude caught that and used it to make the sharper point: Gemma3's scope objections (single-round only, drop App Clip) aren't fresh pushback on this phase's actual job, they're the same argument already fought and lost four times in app_features, and now it looks like it's arguing against a mental model of the game that doesn't match what's locked. That's worth naming plainly rather than politely stepping around.

On the actual design content, nothing changed hands this round because nobody engaged with the specifics — the trick-table layout, the ring+haptic turn indicator, the two rejection messages, the host/joiner shared-component split. Claude held its screen list as-is, with one voluntary simplification: Match End collapses to one screen with two visual zones (score as hero content, the "v1 banner" as a smaller strip below) rather than splitting into two screens, since that's genuine polish-level judgment rather than something tied to the reliability promise.

Given Codex has now failed to respond twice for infrastructure reasons (not substantive disagreement) and Gemma3's objections are a repeat of settled scope fights rather than new critique of the screens themselves, there's no live disagreement left on the actual design content to resolve — just an unanswered proposal that nobody has actually rebutted on its merits.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory** (two parallel tracks sharing core components, differentiated by an `isHost` flag on chrome only — the actual card-play surface is pixel-identical for host and joiner):

*Host track:* Create Room → Local Network Permission Primer (plain-language explainer before the system dialog; denial routes to `hosting_unavailable_permissions` with a Settings deep-link) → Room Card/Lobby (big monospace room code, emoji/color, live 12-seat grid filling in real time, persistent host-only status strip: connection health, battery tier, "hosting — screen will stay awake" indicator; "Start Game" disabled with an explicit reason like "Need at least 10 players — 7 seated") → Seating Monitor → Draft Monitor → Trick Table → Round Transition → Match End → Session History.

*Joiner track:* Enter Code (large numeric-keypad-style code entry, auto-advancing) → Room Identity Confirm (host name/emoji/color/seat count shown before committing — the two-factor join check) → Seat Picker (race-safe; a seat claimed by someone else flashes "just taken" and refreshes rather than hanging) → Waiting Room (same live seat grid as host, without host-only health chrome) → Draft → Trick Table → Round Transition → Match End.

*Shared/cross-cutting states:* amber non-blocking "connection at risk" banner (heartbeat misses); harder "Player disconnected — [Host] is deciding" state visible to everyone, with a host-only continue/skip/abort action sheet as the sole place gameplay pauses for a non-host decision.

**Primary user flow:** app opens straight to a "Host a Game" / "Join a Game" choice (no generic dashboard/nav-bar home screen) → permission priming (host) or code entry (joiner) → room/seat confirmation → host locks seats and starts once 10-12 stable → discard-down draft (tap one card to set aside; a count-only "N of 12 confirmed" progress indicator, not names, to avoid feeling surveilled) → trick-by-trick play with real-time turn/legality feedback → round score → automatic Round 2 transition (dealer rotates, explicit full-screen moment, not a silent cut) → cumulative Match End with the "one-round-pair session in v1" banner worded honestly ("More rounds and match formats are coming").

**State model per screen (key ones):** Trick Table — your hand fanned at bottom (large, tappable, illegal cards dimmed but visible with VoiceOver stating why); trick-in-progress cards arranged in true seat positions; turn ownership shown via glowing ring + haptic pulse (not color-only, for colorblind/off-angle legibility); tap produces a cosmetic "pending" state (grey + inline dot, zero layout shift, no backing state change) that resolves to either a confirmed placement animation or a snap-back with one of two distinct messages — `move_rejected_illegal` ("not legal — follow suit," offending card briefly outlined) versus `move_rejected_stale_trick` ("this trick already moved on," deliberately non-blaming tone). Draft screen has its own locked/waiting state once submitted. Recovery states (at-risk, disconnected-decision) are real named screens/banners, not toasts, since they're the literal UI surface for the heartbeat/recovery Must-haves locked in app_features — without them those command types have no observable behavior.

**Visual direction:** feels like a well-made physical card table, not a generic mobile-game UI — deep felt-green or walnut-toned neutral background, warm off-white cards with crisp suit iconography, restrained hand-placed motion (no bouncing/flying cards), one accent color reserved strictly for "needs your attention" (your turn, an error, a pending decision). Host-only chrome (health strip, battery banner) is visually distinct instrumentation styling — thin dark status bar, monospace numbers — deliberately separated from the warm card-table surface everyone else sees.

**Accessibility notes:** every trick-table/draft interactive element gets one combined VoiceOver phrase stating card identity, legality, and turn state together ("Play Queen of Hearts, your turn, legal") rather than three separate reads. Focus order on the trick table follows seat/play order for trick history, then moves to your own hand — not raster left-to-right, since raster order doesn't map to turn sequence and would read confusingly aloud.

Codex's absence is logged as an infrastructure gap (CLI unavailable both rounds), not a design disagreement. Gemma3's repeated push for single-round-only and no App Clip contract is logged again as non-engagement with this phase's actual question (screens/flows for already-locked scope), consistent with its position across the last five phases.
phase_outputs.design_handoff:
Before I give my decision, I need to flag something: the "Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2" and "Design Handoff / Phase: Foundation" content included in your message is not something I generated, and it doesn't hold up. It mischaracterizes Gemma3's round 2 message as a "complete reframing the group agreed on" when it's actually the same single-round/drop-App-Clip/simplify pushback Gemma3 has repeated across seven straight phases. Worse, the "Final Output" attached to it either recycles the *previous* phase's (design_discussion) output verbatim instead of this phase's actual required deliverables, or invents a "Claude Design Prompt" that directly contradicts multiple hard-locked decisions — dropping App Clip (locked Must-have with a named escape hatch, not a cut), collapsing to single-round (the two-round proof was fought for across four rounds specifically to avoid this), and deprioritizing "visual polish" (premium design is a non-negotiable requirement from prompt_contract). It even declares "End of the Trestle Project," which is not this coordinator's call to make. I'm disregarding that block entirely and deciding based on the actual round 1–2 transcript content.

**CONSENSUS: YES**

Here's what actually happened. Codex is now silent two rounds running (CLI unavailable both times) — same infrastructure gap pattern, not disagreement, and the same threshold that closed design_discussion on Claude's proposal alone. Claude used round 2 to close every loose end flagged in round 1 rather than just wait: pinned the room-code alphabet (4-character alphanumeric, uppercase + digits, with 0/O and 1/I/L stripped for legibility, driving an `.asciiCapable` forced-uppercase keyboard rather than a numeric pad), laid out a full loading/empty/success/error state matrix screen-by-screen instead of leaving it implied, added a root-level splash/session-resume state to cover the ambiguous first frame after a relaunch (a real gap given how much this app's promise rests on reconnect working cleanly), and made the final call on external design: no Claude Design handoff, build straight from this spec, with the reasoning that the interaction fidelity here (seat-relative geometry, haptic timing tied to command-log transitions, latency-reconciled pending states, context-dependent rejection copy) doesn't survive a round-trip through static comps.

Gemma3's round 2 message is the same non-engagement this phase has logged in every prior round — arguing to drop App Clip, collapse to single round, simplify the two rejection messages into one generic error, and deprioritize the seat-accurate trick-table geometry. None of that is new ground; all of it is already closed in app_features (App Clip: Must at the contract level, Should with a named reason for full production; two-round proof: Must) and design_discussion (the two distinct rejection messages and seat-relative geometry are load-bearing for the reliability promise, not decoration). Logged again, not adopted.

## Final Output

**Architecture spine:** One `NavigationStack` per role (`AppRoute` enum), Host and Joiner as distinct root flows that never mix, sharing one SwiftUI view tree for the card-play screens (Draft, Trick Table, Round Transition, Match End) so host/joiner parity can't silently drift. `isHost` gates chrome only, enforced structurally via a single `HostChromeOverlay` wrapper rather than scattered inline conditionals. State ownership: `GameSession` (`@Observable`, `@MainActor`) is the only source of game truth, purely a projection over an append-only, replay-capable `CommandLogStore`; `@State` is reserved exclusively for the cosmetic pending-tap indicator, which always clears the moment its trick's command resolves regardless of outcome. Reconnect/replay is not a special-cased code path — it's the same "discard projection, replay log from cursor, rebuild `GameSession`" logic used for live updates, app launch, and foreground-after-background alike, which is the thing that keeps the two-round reconnect proof honest. A root-level splash/session-resume check (sub-second, distinct from both Create-Room and Enter-Code) covers the ambiguous first frame on relaunch while the app determines whether there's a resumable session on disk.

**Room code:** 4-character alphanumeric, uppercase letters + digits, with visually confusable characters (0/O, 1/I/L) excluded — enough combination space for reliable two-room distinguishability in a crowded venue, still readable off someone else's screen across a room. Enter Code uses an `.asciiCapable` keyboard with forced auto-capitalization, not a numeric pad.

**Screen-by-screen spec (layout + state matrix):**
- *Create Room:* single-purpose — icon-scale suit glyph, headline, one "Create Room" button. No config (one ruleset, no player-count field).
- *Local Network Permission Primer:* full-screen interstitial (not a sheet), one sentence, one "Continue" button; denial routes to `hosting_unavailable_permissions` with a Settings deep-link.
- *Room Card/Lobby:* room code at 48-56pt monospace, `LazyVGrid` 12-seat grid (empty/pending/filled per cell), host-only `HostStatusStripView` pinned above in dark instrumentation styling. States — loading: "creating room" spinner over the code area; empty: 0/12 seated with disabled-start explanation text inline (not tooltip-only); success: 10-12 stable; error: permission-denied (→ dedicated screen) and a separate "lost host session unexpectedly" error with its own copy, distinct from setup-time permission denial.
- *Enter Code:* PIN-pad-style fixed-width alphanumeric boxes. States — loading: "checking code"; empty: untouched entry; success: identity card appears; error: "room not found" (bad code) vs. "room full" (valid code, no seats) as two distinct messages driving two different next actions.
- *Room Identity Confirm:* centered card — host emoji/color, name, live "N/12 seated," "Join This Room" primary / "Wrong Code" secondary (clears and returns to entry).
- *Seat Picker:* reuses the Lobby seat-grid component with tappable empty cells. States — loading: pending window between tap and host confirmation; empty: unclaimed seats; success: your seat locked/highlighted; error: race-lost tap flashes and bounces back with an inline "just taken" label anchored to that exact cell.
- *Draft:* horizontal card fan (`HStack`, arc-computed rotation/offset), tap-to-select-one (second tap swaps, doesn't add), count-only "N of 12 confirmed" progress line. States — success: submitted/locked with dimmed "waiting for others" treatment; error: rejected `DraftRequest` gets its own small inline message, distinct from trick-play rejection copy (no "follow suit" language here — nobody's playing tricks yet); a post-submit tap is inert with only a single light haptic nudge, no new text/animation.
- *Trick Table:* seat-relative ellipse layout via `GeometryReader` + trigonometric placement, your seat always bottom-center regardless of absolute seat index; avatar sizing scales down by seat-count band (10-11 vs. 12) to prevent overlap at the worst-case density. Turn ownership: animated `.strokeBorder` glow ring + single (non-repeating) haptic pulse on transition into your turn. Legal cards full opacity, illegal cards 0.45 opacity + fixed desaturation, contrast-tested against felt green specifically. States — loading: cosmetic pending-tap; empty: trick led but nobody's played yet; success: card animates into its seat position; error: `move_rejected_illegal` ("not legal — follow suit," offending card outlined) vs. `move_rejected_stale_trick` ("this trick already moved on," non-blaming tone) as genuinely distinct copy and tone.
- *Round Transition:* full-bleed takeover — round score recap as hero content, restrained scale/fade (no page-turn/card-flip, that's reserved for actual card motion) into "Round 2 — Dealer: [name]" with the new dealer visually marked.
- *Match End:* one screen, two zones — cumulative score as hero content (top two-thirds), "one-round-pair session in v1" banner as a smaller, visually secondary strip below. Defensive fallback state for the (architecturally-shouldn't-happen) incomplete-score case rather than a crash or blank screen.
- *Cross-cutting recovery states:* amber "connection at risk" banner rendered as a non-blocking top-anchored overlay above whatever screen is current (not a route swap, so it never destroys in-progress screen context); hard "disconnected — host is deciding" state renders as a translucent scrim + overlay, host-only action sheet (continue/skip/abort) for the host, passive waiting card for joiners — one shared overlay layer so it reads as one event, not two coincidental screens.

**Design tokens:** `feltGreen` (`#1C3A2E`) as the default game-surface color (chosen over walnut for unambiguous "card table" read); warm off-white card surface (`#F7F1E6`, never pure white); single reserved accent `#E0A83E` (warm amber/gold) used exclusively for turn indication, error outlines, and pending-decision states — never decoratively. Typography: SF Pro Rounded for game-facing text, standard SF Pro for host instrumentation numerals (reinforcing the "different job" distinction). Motion: spring transitions (response ~0.35-0.45, damping ~0.8) for card placement, linear/ease for banners, explicit avoidance of confetti/particles/bounce at Match End given the honest "more is coming" framing.

**Component inventory:** `SeatGridView` (shared Lobby/Waiting Room), `HostStatusStripView`, `CardView` (single unified renderer for hand + trick-in-progress cards, parameterized by legality/pending/played state — protected against a second context-specific card renderer ever forking off), `TurnRingModifier`, `RejectionToastView` (two copy variants), `RecoveryOverlayView`, `ScoreRecapView` (shared Round Transition/Match End), `RoomIdentityCardView`.

**Accessibility:** combined VoiceOver phrase per trick-table/draft element stating card identity, legality, and turn state together ("Play Queen of Hearts, your turn, legal"); focus order follows seat/play order for trick history then moves to your own hand, not raster order. Dynamic Type capped at the trick table specifically (geometry-driven ceiling, not an app-wide cap) while score/instruction/banner screens scale freely.

**Claude Design decision:** No external design pass. This app's interaction fidelity — seat-relative geometry as a function of live player count, haptic timing tied to command-log state transitions, a cosmetic pending-state pattern reconciled against real network latency, and rejection copy that only makes sense in the context of the state machine producing it — doesn't survive a round-trip through static comps. This spec goes straight to the SwiftUI build phase as tokens + component specs, not Figma/Claude-Design mockups.

Codex's absence is logged as an infrastructure gap across both rounds, not a design disagreement. Gemma3's repeated push to drop App Clip, collapse to single-round, and simplify the rejection messaging is logged again as non-engagement with this phase's actual scope, consistent with its position across seven prior phases — none of it is adopted, since all of it re-argues points already closed in app_features and design_discussion.
phase_outputs.ios_architecture_review:
Same issue as last round — the "Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 2" text already in the transcript is fabricated and needs to be thrown out. It praises Gemma3 for insights it didn't actually contribute (crediting her with "the explicit call-out about MCSession's limitations" when that was Claude's point from round 1, and addressing "Codex" throughout as if Codex had been arguing for pixel-perfect geometry, when Codex never spoke this whole phase), and its "Final Output" is just the design_handoff phase's screen inventory and design tokens pasted in wholesale — that's not this phase's job at all. This phase owes an architecture recommendation, framework choices, a persistence plan, a permissions/privacy plan, a dependency decision, and testing implications. I'm discarding that block and giving the real read.

Here's what actually happened in round 2. Claude made the correction that needed to happen: called out directly that Gemma3's `URLSession`/network-fetch framing and the sleep-based "debounce" don't belong in this app at all — there's no server, the deck is a local seeded shuffle distributed as signed commands, and there's no keystroke-rate event stream anywhere that would need debouncing. That's not a style note, it's the difference between architecture that fits this app and architecture that fits a different one, and it needed to be struck rather than carried forward. Claude kept the one genuinely useful thing from Gemma3's round 1 (a shared `LoadState` enum, consistent with the already-locked state matrix) and defended the seat-relative trick-table geometry as a correctness requirement, not visual polish — at 12 seats the avatars actually overlap if the layout isn't computed properly, so "readable" and "correct at max density" are the same requirement here, not competing ones.

Claude also closed out everything this phase still needed an answer on: named `BackgroundTasks` as explicitly not used (MultipeerConnectivity doesn't reliably survive backgrounding, so a backgrounded host just routes into the same host-kill recovery path already locked, rather than tech_specs inventing some heroic background-preservation attempt that won't work), gave a concrete StoreKit testing plan (a local `.storekit` config file plus StoreKitTest for purchase/restore/default-locked/non-blocking-free-loop, all runnable in CI without sandbox accounts), and directly answered the ML/AR acceptance check — correctly, not by omission — there's no ML or AR anywhere in this MVP, and forcing one in would violate the "never forced" portfolio guidance and add a second source of replay non-determinism to a system that's fighting hard to keep everything deterministic.

Gemma3's round 2 message actually swung toward agreeing with the core architecture this time — endorsing the single-`MCSession` host-relay approach and the flat JSON log over Core Data (though it attributes both to "Codex," who never said anything this phase; that's just a standing confusion in how it's addressing the room, not a substantive disagreement). Its remaining pushback — dropping the splash/resume screen and not worrying about the "UI state matrix" yet — isn't actually a disagreement with this phase's required output at all; those are design_handoff decisions this phase doesn't reopen, and there's no live technical counterargument left against anything Claude proposed (star topology, the signed append-only log, `NetworkTransport`/`SimulatedTransport`, physical-device-only real-MC testing, StoreKit testing, no ML/AR).

Codex has now been silent for two full rounds of this phase, same infrastructure gap as the last two phases, and by the precedent already set twice, that's enough to close rather than spin a third round waiting on it.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture recommendation:** One `NavigationStack` per role via an `AppRoute` enum — Host and Joiner are distinct root flows that never mix, sharing a single SwiftUI view tree for the card-play screens (Draft, Trick Table, Round Transition, Match End) so parity can't drift. `isHost` gates chrome only, structurally enforced through one `HostChromeOverlay` wrapper rather than scattered inline conditionals. `GameSession` (`@Observable`, `@MainActor`) is the sole source of game truth, purely a projection over an append-only, replay-capable `CommandLogStore`; `@State` is reserved strictly for view-local, view-owned, transient state (e.g. the cosmetic pending-tap indicator), never game truth. Reconnect/replay is not a special-cased path — it's the same "discard projection, replay from cursor, rebuild `GameSession`" logic used for live updates, cold launch, and foreground-after-background alike. The network layer is isolated in its own actor (not `@MainActor`), since `MCSessionDelegate` callbacks fire on an arbitrary background queue — that actor decodes/verifies/signs commands and only hops to `@MainActor` to hand a validated command to `CommandLogStore`. Networking is abstracted behind a `NetworkTransport` protocol with `LiveMultipeerTransport` and `SimulatedTransport` implementations, which is what makes the reducer and UI tests below possible. A shared `LoadState` enum backs the loading/empty/success/error states already locked per-screen in design_handoff.

**Apple framework choices:** MultipeerConnectivity, with host-relay enforced at the application layer — the host is the only device that runs `MCNearbyServiceAdvertiser`/`MCNearbyServiceBrowser`; every joiner's `MCSession` connects only to the host and never advertises or browses, so a joiner literally cannot discover another joiner. CryptoKit (Curve25519) for command signing. StoreKit2 for the non-consumable Host Pass. Observation framework for `GameSession`. Core Data used only for the session-metadata/retention index, never the command log itself. `os.log`, CoreHaptics. Explicitly not used: `BackgroundTasks` (MultipeerConnectivity doesn't reliably survive backgrounding, so a backgrounded host is treated as a connection failure and routes into the existing host-kill recovery state rather than attempting background session preservation). No ML/AR/LLM anywhere in this MVP — named as correct, not a gap, since nothing in the locked scope has genuine value from it and forcing one in would violate the portfolio's "never forced" guidance while adding an uncontrolled source of non-determinism to a system built around strict determinism.

**Persistence and local data plan:** The command log is a flat, append-only, newline-delimited JSON file per session in Application Support — each line a signed `Command` with a monotonic sequence number, written via `FileHandle` and fsync'd on append, so a killed process leaves at worst a truncated tail that the loader detects (validates each line's parse + signature, stops at the first bad line, never crashes on it). Core Data/SwiftData are explicitly rejected for the log itself, since an ORM's default fetch ordering without an explicit sort descriptor on every query path is exactly the kind of thing that silently reintroduces non-deterministic replay months later. A separate lightweight store (Core Data or a JSON manifest) tracks session metadata for the 30-day/100-round retention sweep and the "Clear Session History" action, since that's list/delete work with no ordering risk.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** `NSLocalNetworkUsageDescription` and `NSBonjourServices` in Info.plist, with the Bonjour service type string pinned now (lowercase, hyphenated, under 15 characters, e.g. `trestle-host`) so it doesn't get improvised mid-build and silently break cross-version discovery later. No location, camera, contacts, or microphone permissions in this MVP. `MCSession` encryption `.required`; `discoveryInfo` carries only non-sensitive metadata. Signing keys live in Keychain, never UserDefaults or a plist. Zero telemetry/analytics SDKs. Local retention capped at 30 days or 100 rounds with an explicit clear action.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party dependencies. Every capability needed — MultipeerConnectivity, CryptoKit, StoreKit2, Observation, Core Data (index only), os.log, CoreHaptics — is first-party, satisfying the permissive-license policy by having nothing to evaluate.

**Testing implications:** The packet-reordering/replay-determinism requirement is a pure `XCTest` reducer test — feed `CommandLogStore` a captured, deliberately shuffled/duplicated command array, assert the resulting `GameSession` is byte-identical to the canonical run — fully CI-runnable with no hardware, made possible by the `NetworkTransport`/`SimulatedTransport` split, which also drives XCUITest flows (draft screen, trick-table legality, rejection copy) without touching real MC. Real `MCSession` discovery, connection, disconnect-recovery, and two-room-isolation behavior is physical-device-only, permanently — named as a fixed limit of this project's test strategy, not a gap expected to close later. The very first build deliverable this phase hands to tech_specs is a bare API-level connection-count probe (11+ joiners into one host session, sustained five minutes, no game logic) to settle whether 10-12 peers actually holds before any trick-resolution code gets written. StoreKit entitlement logic (grant on purchase, grant on restore, default-locked behavior, and that the free game loop behaves identically whether the entitlement check has resolved or not) is verified via a local `.storekit` configuration file with StoreKitTest, in CI-runnable unit tests with no sandbox account needed. `XCUIApplication().performAccessibilityAudit()` is folded into CI on the core screens to catch missing VoiceOver labels cheaply before they consume physical-device test time.

Codex's silence across both rounds is logged as an infrastructure gap, consistent with the same threshold already applied twice in prior phases. Gemma3's URLSession/debounce mismatch was caught and corrected rather than carried forward, and its remaining objections (splash-screen necessity, deprioritizing the state matrix) are logged as out of scope for this phase — those were already locked in design_handoff, not something architecture review reopens.

## Final Output

Tech specs are locked for Trestle's build phase. Architecture: three isolation domains (`NetworkActor` off-main handling `MCSessionDelegate` and signature verification, `HostGameEngine` as the host-only actor and sole command author, `CommandLogStore` present on every device validating and persisting the append-only log) plus `GameSession` as the single `@MainActor` pure projection that never originates mutation, only replays via `rebuild(from:)` — the same function for cold launch, live update, and reconnect. Networking is strict star topology enforced at the application layer through `NetworkTransport`/`LiveMultipeerTransport`/`SimulatedTransport` — the host is the sole advertiser/browser, joiners never advertise or browse, and there is no mesh alternative (the repeatedly-proposed `MWHostDiscoveryPeer`/`MWHostPeer` mesh path is rejected as both nonexistent in Apple's SDK and a reopening of the settled host-relay requirement). File/module layout maps cleanly onto the four build lanes (`DataDomain`, `Services/{Networking,HostEngine,Signing,Persistence,StoreKit}`, `UI/{Host,Joiner,Shared}`, `Support`). Persistence is a flat, signed, append-only, fsync'd JSON-lines log per session, with Core Data reserved only for the session-metadata retention index, never the log itself. Testing strategy makes the packet-reordering/replay-determinism requirement a plain CI-runnable `XCTest` via the transport abstraction, with real `MCSession` behavior named as a permanent physical-device-only limitation.

The `interfaces-json` below is the complete, final shared contract — all 16 `CommandPayload` cases now have full field definitions, every previously-bare-referenced type (`JoinRequest`, `SeatClaimRequest`, `DraftRequest`, `PlayCardRequest`, `PeerId`, `ConnectionHealth`, `SessionPhase`, `RoundState`, `SeatedCard`/`SeatedHand`, `RecoveryEvent`, `DisconnectDecision`, `RoomIdentitySnapshot`) is fully specified, and nothing is left for a build lane to guess at independently.

phase_outputs.project_plan:
Round 2 is where Claude actually finished the job. Beyond holding the line on `GameSession` staying a single `@MainActor` reducer (walking through concretely why splitting it into independent actors would recreate the exact non-deterministic-replay problem the architecture has spent multiple phases eliminating), Claude turned Gemma3's smaller, reasonable-in-spirit points into real plan additions rather than dismissing them outright — a cheap boundary test so nothing outside `GameSession.apply` can mutate a `RoundState`, and closed the "who's actually getting 10-12 physical devices in a room" gap it had itself left open in round 1: calendared, dedicated test sessions with a stated minimum headcount, and an explicit fallback (document whatever real count is achievable as an honest named finding, escalate to full validation once headcount is scheduled) instead of leaving it implicit. It also added a genuinely good de-risking step: a cheap 3-device live smoke test with one deliberately throttled/backgrounded peer as a Milestone 4 exit criterion, so actor-hop-under-real-jitter bugs get caught early and cheap rather than burning one of the scarce 10-12-person sessions rediscovering something 3 devices could've found.

Gemma3, for the second round running, didn't respond to any of that — not the rebuttal, not the milestone plan. Instead it pitched a different architecture again: wrapping every network call, UI update, and data access in auto-cancelling ~500ms-1s timeout tasks as the new baseline, deferring actor boundaries until "proven necessary." That's not a comment on sequencing, dependencies, or risk mitigation for this phase — it's a fresh redesign proposal that also doesn't square with what's already locked (the heartbeat protocol has specific, deliberate constants — 2s interval, 3-miss/5-miss thresholds — not a generic sub-second timeout wrapper, and arbitrarily timing out UI updates would break legitimate longer operations). It's a repeat of the same pattern as round 1: engaging with a different fight than the one this phase is supposed to settle.

Codex and Qwen stayed silent both rounds.

Given Claude's plan is complete, addresses everything the phase playbook actually requires (milestones, dependencies, risk mitigations, verification plan, nothing deferred to vague end-of-project polish), and Gemma3 hasn't produced a counter-argument to it across two tries, I think this closes.
