## Final Output

**Milestones (in dependency order, not role order):**

- **M0 — Scaffold & compile, zero game logic.** Xcode project/target/bundle-id/signing set up, folder structure mapped 1:1 to interfaces-json's four owning_lanes, every contract type stubbed and compiling under Swift 6 strict concurrency. Acceptance bar is purely "it typechecks," including across the actor boundaries (`NetworkActor` ↔ `HostGameEngine` ↔ `@MainActor GameSession`) — this is where Sendable/actor-isolation mistakes get caught cheaply instead of three weeks in.
- **M1 — Connectivity probe (go/no-go gate).** The bare API-level test named back in ios_architecture_review: one host, 11+ joiners, MCSession only, no game logic, sustained 5 minutes, run repeatedly. Runs as a dedicated, calendared test session with a stated minimum headcount (10-12 people with personal iPhones, or a device lab making up the gap). If that count can't hold reliably, the named fallback is: test at whatever real count is achievable, record the actual ceiling as a dated, explicit finding, and only re-attempt full 10-12 validation once a session with that headcount is scheduled — never a silent scope-down. Can run in parallel with M2/M3 since it doesn't block pure-logic work.
- **M2 — Command core.** `CommandLogStore`, `CommandSigner`, `KeychainKeyStore`, `SimulatedTransport`, `GameSession.rebuild`. Fully CI-testable, no real MC needed. Two enforced guardrails become build-breaking CI checks, not spec-doc sentences: (1) a lint step failing the build on `Dictionary`/`Set<` usage in DataDomain or the reducer path outside the named exceptions (Keychain, Core Data metadata); (2) a boundary test asserting nothing outside `GameSession.apply` can construct or mutate a `RoundState`.
- **M3 — Game rules & engine.** `GameRules` (legality, trick resolution, scoring) and `HostGameEngine` wired to `CommandLogStore` via `SimulatedTransport`. Discard-down draft, lead rotation, and the two distinct rejection paths (`moveRejectedIllegal` vs `moveRejectedStaleTrick`) proven via unit tests against fabricated — including deliberately reordered/duplicated — command sequences. Depends on M2, not M1.
- **M4 — Live wiring.** `LiveMultipeerTransport` plugged into `HostGameEngine`/`CommandLogStore`. Treated as a real integration checkpoint with budgeted repair time, not a formality. Exit criterion includes a 3-device live smoke test with one deliberately throttled/backgrounded peer, specifically to catch actor-hop-under-real-jitter problems here rather than for the first time during the expensive M8 gauntlet.
- **M5 — primary_ui screens.** Buildable against M0's types and SimulatedTransport-backed fake states as soon as M0 lands, in parallel with M1-M4. Accessibility (VoiceOver labels, focus order, `performAccessibilityAudit()`) is part of each screen's own "done," not a late separate pass. A named re-validation task runs after M4 lands to check screens against real, jittery, live-timed transitions rather than only clean synthetic ones.
- **M6 — polish_resilience.** Heartbeat monitor UI, recovery overlay, rejection toast, host status strip, battery/thermal banners. Depends on M3 (commands rendered) and M4 (live signal timing). Includes an explicit 60+ minute real-hardware session task to watch the battery tier actually transition green→yellow→red, not just a code review of the threshold constants.
- **M7 — Two-round match proof.** Dealer rotation, cumulative score, reconnect between round 1's score post and round 2's redeal. Depends on M3; proven first via SimulatedTransport, then confirmed live.
- **M8 — Physical-device production-readiness gauntlet.** Depends on M4, M5, M6, M7. Five distinct, separately recorded test protocols (not one line item): three consecutive clean 10-12-player games, host-kill-mid-round recovery, disconnect-mid-draft recovery, packet-reordering replay check, two-room isolation under simultaneous churn. "Ran it once and it seemed fine" explicitly does not satisfy this.
- **M9 — HostPassEntitlement/StoreKit scaffolding**, fields-only. No gameplay dependency, can slot in any time after M0. `.storekit` test config built early so entitlement resolution gets tested against the real free loop as it's built.
- **M10 — Final review** against the production-readiness definition already locked in prompt_contract — not a newly invented rubric.

**Key dependencies:** M1 and M2/M3 run in parallel (no shared blocking). M3 depends on M2. M4 depends on M3 and a working M1 (or its documented fallback ceiling). M5 starts at M0 in parallel with everything, with a mandatory re-check after M4. M6 depends on M3 and M4. M7 depends on M3. M8 depends on M4, M5, M6, M7 all landing. M9 is independent after M0.

**Risk mitigations:** M1 as a real gate with a named decision procedure and fallback for the 10-12-peer question; CI-enforced (not just documented) no-Dictionary/no-Set rule and RoundState-mutation-boundary test; accessibility built per-screen, not batched at the end; a cheap 3-device throttled-peer smoke test at M4 to protect the scarce, hard-to-schedule 10-12-person M8 sessions from absorbing bugs that didn't need that many bodies to find; battery/thermal behavior verified with a real long-duration session, not just constant review; device-sourcing for M1/M8 treated as a named scheduling dependency with a stated headcount and an honest fallback, not an implicit assumption.

**Verification plan:** CI-runnable reducer/replay-determinism tests from M2 onward; XCUITest accessibility audits per UI screen as built; a 3-device jitter smoke test at M4; a 60+ minute battery-tier test at M6; and the five-protocol physical-device gauntlet at M8 as the final production-readiness check, each protocol with its own pass/fail record rather than one generic "tested on device" line.

Codex and Qwen's silence across both rounds is logged as an infrastructure gap, consistent with this project's established precedent. Gemma3's round 1 and round 2 proposals (actor-split redesign, then a timeout-wrapped-task redesign) are logged as non-engagement with this phase's actual deliverable, not adopted.

## Final Output

Trestle's build splits into four local Swift Packages inside one thin Xcode project — `Packages/DataDomain`, `Packages/Services`, `Packages/PrimaryUI`, `Packages/PolishResilience` — one per fixed lane, so adding a file never touches `project.pbxproj` and never creates a merge conflict across lanes. `data_domain` owns every contract type and the `GameSession` reducer (the only thing allowed to mutate game state). `services_utilities` owns signing, persistence, networking (`NetworkTransport`/`SimulatedTransport`/`LiveMultipeerTransport`), the host engine, heartbeat, session retention, and StoreKit scaffolding. `primary_ui` owns the actual screens and shared components, built against hand-authored preview fixtures so it never has to wait on `services_utilities`. `polish_resilience` owns the cross-cutting resilience layer (recovery overlay, rejection toast, host status strip, haptics, accessibility audit) and is the lane responsible for actually writing up the physical-device test protocols.

Merge order: data_domain first (everyone imports it), then services_utilities and primary_ui in parallel, then polish_resilience last. Branches are `lane/data-domain`, `lane/services-utilities`, `lane/primary-ui`, `lane/polish-resilience`, cut from `main` right after the scaffold task lands alone, with frequent rebasing rather than long-lived branches.

Forbidden edit zone: `TrestleApp/App.swift` may only be touched by exactly three tasks across the whole build — T-001 (scaffold stub), T-020 (routing), and T-034 (live-wiring + the one place PrimaryUI and PolishResilience are composed together) — never anywhere else. `primary_ui` never imports `services_utilities` directly (it builds against its own fixtures); `primary_ui` never imports `polish_resilience` directly either — that composition happens only through an injected chrome slot, wired once in T-034.

The full 37-task backlog below is the assignment — every task has an owner lane, an explicit file list, acyclic dependencies, and acceptance criteria a build worker can self-verify against, tracing directly back to the locked interfaces-json and project_plan milestones.

phase_outputs.implementation_readiness_gate:
CONSENSUS: YES

Round two didn't bring anything new to weigh — Claude held its five findings from round one, sharpened each into a concrete fix (a specific hashing scheme for the room-code/nonce problem, the exact four screens to add to the accessibility task, one precise acceptance line for the App Clip hook, plain language on the headcount risk, and an exact phrase to pin "a game" to), and even voluntarily softened its own App Clip ask from "new task" down to "one acceptance line," which is the kind of self-correction that suggests it's actually trying to close this cleanly rather than pad its position. Gemma3 came back a second time with the same move as round one — generic scale/failure-mode/architecture-review concerns, no engagement with any of the five specific items on the table, no acknowledgment that its "CommandLogFileStore double-ownership" complaint was pointing at a bug fixed two phases ago. Codex and Qwen were silent both rounds, same infrastructure gap seen throughout this project. Given the two-round non-engagement threshold this project has applied consistently everywhere else, and that Claude's five fixes are concrete, mutually consistent, and nobody has raised a real technical objection to any of them, this closes now on Claude's findings.

## Final Output

**Readiness verdict:** Conditionally ready. The architecture, package split, command-log design, and task backlog locked in prior phases are sound and build workers can start on M0/M2/M3 immediately. Five specific gaps need to close before M1's connectivity probe and M4's live wiring begin, since two of them would force rework of already-built networking code if discovered later.

**Cross-phase consistency check:** The chain from prompt_contract through task_assignments is coherent except for the five items below. No contradictions found between product brief, design handoff, iOS architecture, tech specs, or the task backlog beyond these — the four-package lane split, the no-client-mutation rule, the signed replayable command log, and the milestone sequencing all hold together as written.

**Build blockers and fixes:**
1. **Room-code-to-host filtering gap (blocks T-014/M1/M4).** Nothing currently lets a joiner's device pick the right host when two rooms broadcast nearby, because `discoveryInfo`'s `serviceNonce` was previously unspecified in derivation. Fix: `serviceNonce` is derived as a keyed hash of the room code at room-creation time — the host advertises it, the joiner's browser independently computes the same hash from the code they typed, and only invites a peer whose advertised nonce matches. This gets written into tech_specs and added as a concrete acceptance criterion on T-014 before that task starts.
2. **Accessibility coverage gap (T-033).** Add `EnterCodeView`, `PermissionPrimerView`, `SeatPickerView`, and the recovery overlay/rejection toast to T-033's audited screen list, alongside the already-covered lobby/draft/trick-table/match-end.
3. **App Clip contract-readiness hook has no enforcement (T-012).** Add one acceptance line to T-012: a `ClipAdapterStub` in the test target constructs a `JoinRequest` from a fabricated Clip-style payload and proves it drives the same seat-claim state machine as the manual-code path, with zero changes to `JoinRequest`'s shape.
4. **Device-headcount risk isn't named as the top risk.** M1 and M8 both require a dedicated session with 10-12 real people and real phones, twice, and nothing has that scheduled yet. This gate names it plainly: the single biggest open risk to shipping the promised product is non-technical — getting bodies in a room — and the realistic near-term outcome, absent scheduling before M4, is the fallback path already defined in project_plan (document the actual achievable ceiling as a dated, honest finding, not a silent scope cut).
5. **Scope-language clarification.** For this MVP, "a game" in the production-readiness bar (including the M8 gauntlet's "three consecutive clean games") means the two-round shrunk-hand match locked in next_steps_small — not a full 13-card multi-round match. This is stated explicitly so nothing downstream is read as promising more than what's shipping.

**Final scope for build:** Unchanged from project_plan and task_assignments' locked 29-task backlog (T-001 through T-036 with lane-based numbering gaps), with the five amendments above folded into T-012, T-014, and T-033, and items 4 and 5 carried forward as documented, named constraints rather than code changes.

**Acceptance criteria checklist (this gate's own bar):**
- [x] Build phase can start without inventing product/design/architecture decisions — yes, only the five named amendments are additive to already-locked specs.
- [x] Every major workflow has an acceptance criterion — confirmed across the backlog, with the room-code filter and Clip-adapter proof now explicitly closing the two workflows that lacked one.
- [x] Testing responsibilities are explicit — CI-runnable reducer/replay tests, XCUITest accessibility audits (now with corrected screen coverage), the M1 probe, the M4 3-device smoke test, the M7 two-round proof, and the M8 five-protocol physical gauntlet all have named owners and pass/fail record requirements.
- [x] Named blockers resolved or explicitly downgraded, not silently dropped — all five items above are either a concrete fix or an honestly-named contingency, per this project's own decision rules.

Gemma3's round 1 and round 2 contributions are logged as non-engagement with this phase's actual gate questions — general architecture/process concerns and a stale bug report, not responses to the five specific findings — consistent with the same pattern across prior phases. Codex and Qwen's silence across both rounds is logged as the same infrastructure gap applied throughout this project.
